stem: A patient fractures the surgical neck of the humerus in a fall. Which nerve is most at risk and what is the characteristic sensory deficit?
options: Radial nerve — loss of sensation over the posterior forearm
Axillary nerve — loss of sensation over the lateral shoulder (regimental badge area)
Musculocutaneous nerve — loss of sensation over the lateral forearm
Median nerve — loss of sensation over the lateral 3.5 digits
Ulnar nerve — loss of sensation over the medial 1.5 digits
explanation: The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus. Injury causes paralysis of deltoid and teres minor, and sensory loss over the regimental badge area of the lateral shoulder. The radial nerve is at risk at the spiral groove of the mid-humerus.

stem: Which carpal bone articulates with the radius to form the radiocarpal joint?
explanation: The radiocarpal joint is formed by the distal radius articulating with the scaphoid and lunate. The triquetrum articulates with the fibrocartilaginous articular disc associated with the distal ulna.

stem: Which muscle is the only component of quadriceps femoris that can also flex the hip?
explanation: Rectus femoris originates from the anterior inferior iliac spine (AIIS) and crosses the hip joint, allowing it to flex the hip. The three vasti originate from the femur and only cross the knee joint.

stem: Name the eight carpal bones in the proximal and distal rows.
answer: Proximal row (lateral to medial): scaphoid / lunate / triquetrum / pisiform. Distal row (lateral to medial): trapezium / trapezoid / capitate / hamate.
explanation: Mnemonic: Some Lovers Try Positions That They Cannot Handle.

stem: A patient's pelvis drops to the left when they lift their left foot during walking. Which muscle and nerve are affected?
options: Left gluteus maximus — inferior gluteal nerve
Right gluteus medius — superior gluteal nerve
Left gluteus medius — superior gluteal nerve
Right gluteus maximus — inferior gluteal nerve
explanation: Trendelenburg sign — when the left foot is lifted, the RIGHT gluteus medius must contract to hold the pelvis level. If the pelvis drops to the left, the right gluteus medius is weak. Supplied by the superior gluteal nerve (L4, L5, S1).

stem: Which structure does the femoral artery pass through to become the popliteal artery?
explanation: The femoral artery travels through the adductor canal then passes through the adductor hiatus — an opening in the tendinous insertion of adductor magnus — to become the popliteal artery posterior to the knee.

stem: The common fibular nerve is most vulnerable at which anatomical location?
options: Winding around the neck of the fibula
explanation: The common fibular nerve winds superficially around the neck of the fibula with minimal soft tissue protection. Injury causes foot drop (loss of dorsiflexion and eversion) and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and anterolateral leg.

stem: Name the contents of the tarsal tunnel from anterior to posterior using the mnemonic.
answer: Tom Dick And Very Nervous Harry: Tibialis posterior / Flexor Digitorum longus / posterior tibial Artery / posterior tibial Vein / tibial Nerve / Flexor Hallucis longus.
explanation: The tarsal tunnel lies posterior to the medial malleolus. Its roof is formed by the flexor retinaculum.

stem: A patient has wrist drop following fracture of the mid-shaft of the humerus. Which nerve is damaged and where?
options: Axillary nerve — surgical neck of humerus
Radial nerve — spiral groove of humerus
explanation: The radial nerve runs in the spiral groove of the humerus and is at risk with mid-shaft humeral fractures. Injury causes wrist drop (loss of wrist and finger extension). Saturday night palsy is caused by the same mechanism from pressure on the arm.

stem: The axillary artery is divided into three parts by which muscle?
explanation: Pectoralis minor divides the axillary artery into: Part 1 (medial to pec minor) / Part 2 (deep to pec minor) / Part 3 (lateral to pec minor). The axillary artery begins at the lateral border of the 1st rib and ends at the inferior border of teres major.

stem: Name the five segments of the brachial plexus in order from proximal to distal.
answer: Roots (C5–T1) → Trunks (upper / middle / lower) → Divisions (anterior / posterior) → Cords (lateral / medial / posterior — named relative to axillary artery) → Terminal branches.
explanation: Mnemonic: Randy Travis Drinks Cold Beer.

stem: A patient has Bell's palsy affecting the right side. Which of the following would you expect on examination?
options: Inability to wrinkle forehead on right — spared on left
Forehead spared bilaterally — only lower face affected on right
Complete loss of all facial expression on the right including forehead
Loss of facial expression on left side only
Bilateral facial weakness with forehead sparing
explanation: Bell's palsy is a lower motor neuron (peripheral) CN7 lesion, affecting ALL ipsilateral facial muscles including the forehead. An upper motor neuron (central) lesion spares the forehead because the upper face has bilateral cortical representation.

stem: Which muscle of mastication protracts the mandible?
explanation: Lateral pterygoid protracts the mandible and opens the jaw. Masseter retracts, temporalis elevates and retracts, medial pterygoid elevates. All muscles of mastication are supplied by the mandibular branch (V3) of the trigeminal nerve. Buccinator is a muscle of facial expression (CN7).

stem: Name the two structures within the carotid sheath alongside the common carotid artery, and state their relative positions.
answer: Internal jugular vein (lateral to artery) and vagus nerve CN10 (posterior, between artery and vein).
explanation: The common carotid artery gives no branches until its terminal bifurcation into internal and external carotid arteries at C4.

stem: Which muscles form the pelvic diaphragm?
explanation: The pelvic diaphragm consists of levator ani and coccygeus only. Piriformis and obturator internus are lateral pelvic wall muscles — not part of the pelvic diaphragm. Functions: support viscera / resist intra-abdominal pressure / continence.

stem: Where do the vaginal and uterine arteries originate, and at what level do the gonadal arteries arise?
options: Internal iliac artery — gonadal arteries at L1
External iliac artery — gonadal arteries at L2
Internal iliac artery — gonadal arteries at L2
Common iliac artery — gonadal arteries at L3
Abdominal aorta — gonadal arteries at L4
explanation: Vaginal and uterine arteries arise from the internal iliac artery (anterior division). Gonadal arteries arise directly from the abdominal aorta at L2.

stem: Name the superficial perineal muscles.
answer: Superficial transverse perineal / ischiocavernosus / bulbospongiosus — same in both sexes.
explanation: Deep perineal muscles: external urethral sphincter (both sexes) + sphincter urethrovaginalis and compressor urethrae (female only).

stem: At which vertebral level does the oesophagus pass through the diaphragm, and what else passes through the same hiatus?
options: T8 — IVC and right phrenic nerve
T10 — anterior and posterior vagal trunks and left gastric vessels
T12 — thoracic duct and azygos vein
explanation: The oesophageal hiatus is at T10 — transmits the oesophagus, anterior and posterior vagal trunks, and oesophageal branches of the left gastric vessels. T8 = IVC (caval opening). T12 = aorta, thoracic duct, azygos vein (aortic hiatus). Mnemonic: I ate (8) ten (10) Eggs At twelve (12).

stem: The intercostal neurovascular bundle runs between which two layers of intercostal muscle?
options: Between the external intercostal and the parietal pleura
explanation: The intercostal neurovascular bundle (VAN — vein, artery, nerve from superior to inferior) runs between the internal and innermost intercostal muscles in the subcostal groove of the upper rib. Needles must be inserted just above the upper border of the lower rib to avoid the bundle.

stem: Name the three movements of the thoracic cage during inspiration and the dimension each increases.
answer: Bucket handle motion of ribs → increases transverse dimension. Pump handle action of sternum → increases AP dimension. Inferior movement of diaphragm → increases vertical dimension.
explanation: All three together increase thoracic volume, reducing intrathoracic pressure and drawing air into the lungs.

topic: Blood Supply to Head, Neck and Limbs
stem: The left common carotid artery arises from which vessel?
options: Directly from the arch of the aorta
explanation: The left common carotid artery arises directly from the arch of the aorta as its second branch. The right common carotid arises from the brachiocephalic trunk (first branch). Both have no branches until their terminal bifurcation.

topic: Blood Supply to Head, Neck and Limbs
stem: Which artery is dominant in the superficial palmar arch?
explanation: The ulnar artery is dominant in the superficial palmar arch. The radial artery is dominant in the deep palmar arch. Both arteries contribute to both arches but one dominates each.

topic: Blood Supply to Head, Neck and Limbs
stem: Trace the arterial supply from the aortic arch to the femoral artery.
answer: Aortic arch → brachiocephalic trunk (right) / directly (left) → common iliac arteries (bifurcation at L4) → external iliac artery → passes inferior to inguinal ligament → femoral artery.
explanation: The internal iliac artery supplies the pelvis and perineum. The external iliac artery becomes the femoral artery as it passes deep to the inguinal ligament at the midpoint between ASIS and pubic symphysis.

stem: Which vein does the left gonadal vein drain into?
explanation: The left gonadal vein drains into the left renal vein. The right gonadal vein drains directly into the IVC. This asymmetry is clinically relevant — a left-sided varicocele may indicate left renal vein compression.

stem: The hepatic portal vein is formed by the union of which two vessels?
options: Superior mesenteric vein and inferior mesenteric vein
Superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein
Inferior mesenteric vein and splenic vein
Superior mesenteric vein and left gastric vein
explanation: The portal vein is formed posterior to the neck of the pancreas by the union of the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein. The inferior mesenteric vein usually joins the splenic vein. Portal blood → liver → hepatic veins → IVC.

stem: What is the function of the pleural cavities?
options: To provide a space for the lungs to expand into during forced expiration
To provide surface tension and cohesion keeping the lung surfaces in contact with the thoracic wall
To lubricate the pericardium during cardiac contraction
To allow gas exchange at the pleural surface
To store lymphatic fluid from the lungs
explanation: The pleural cavities provide surface tension and cohesion to keep the lung surfaces in contact with the thoracic wall. They are potential spaces that can fill with air (pneumothorax), blood (haemothorax), or fluid (pleural effusion).

stem: Why is the left costomediastinal recess larger than the right?
options: The left lung has three lobes compared to two on the right
The cardiac notch of the left lung creates a larger unfilled pleural space
The right lung is larger and fills more of the pleural cavity
The aorta displaces the left lung laterally
The left hemidiaphragm sits higher than the right
explanation: The cardiac notch in the left lung is more pronounced, leaving a larger unfilled space between the costal and mediastinal pleura on the left — the costomediastinal recess. These are potential spaces visible only in the living.

stem: What are the differences between the right and left main bronchi? Why is this clinically significant?
answer: Right main bronchus: wider, shorter, more vertical. Left main bronchus: narrower, longer, more horizontal. Clinical significance: inhaled foreign bodies and aspirated material more commonly lodge in the right bronchus due to its more vertical course.
explanation: The right bronchus divides into 3 lobar bronchi (upper/middle/lower). The left divides into 2 lobar bronchi (upper/lower).

stem: Which landmark divides the superior and inferior mediastinum?
options: The sternal angle at T4/5 intervertebral disc
The level of the clavicles at T1
The level of the 4th rib at T6
explanation: The sternal angle (Angle of Louis) at the T4/5 intervertebral disc divides the superior from inferior mediastinum. It also marks the beginning and end of the aortic arch, carina, and azygos vein drainage into the SVC.

stem: What are the contents of the middle mediastinum?
options: Heart, pericardium, origins of great vessels, tracheal bifurcation and main bronchi
Arch of aorta, SVC, thoracic duct
Sympathetic chains, thoracic duct, vagus nerves
explanation: Middle mediastinum contains: heart, pericardium, origins of great vessels, tracheal bifurcation and main bronchi, and phrenic nerves. Posterior mediastinum contains the thoracic aorta, azygos system, oesophagus, and sympathetic chains.

stem: Into which heart chamber does the majority of venous blood from the heart itself drain, and via what structure?
answer: The right atrium, via the coronary sinus. Small anterior cardiac veins also drain directly into the right atrium.
explanation: The coronary sinus lies in the posterior atrioventricular groove and receives the great, middle, and small cardiac veins before draining into the right atrium.

stem: Which three structures form the nasal septum?
options: Vomer / ethmoid perpendicular plate / nasal bone
Vomer / septal cartilage / ethmoid perpendicular plate
explanation: The nasal septum is formed by: the vomer (posterior inferior) / perpendicular plate of ethmoid (posterior superior) / septal cartilage (anterior). The inferior nasal concha is a separate bone on the lateral wall — not part of the septum.

stem: Which cranial nerve does the recurrent laryngeal nerve branch from, and what does each side loop around?
options: CN9 — right loops around right subclavian / left loops around aortic arch
CN10 — right loops around right subclavian / left loops around aortic arch
CN10 — both loop around the aortic arch
CN11 — right loops around brachiocephalic trunk / left loops around aortic arch
CN12 — right loops around right common carotid / left loops around aortic arch
explanation: The recurrent laryngeal nerve is a branch of the vagus nerve (CN10). The right RLN loops around the right subclavian artery. The left RLN loops around the aortic arch — giving it a longer intrathoracic course and making it vulnerable to mediastinal pathology.

stem: What is the mucociliary escalator and what are the functions of goblet cells and cilia in respiratory epithelium?
answer: The mucociliary escalator is the rhythmic wafting of cilia which moves mucus (containing trapped pathogens and particles) towards the larynx for swallowing. Goblet cells produce mucus. Cilia waft the mucus towards the pharynx.
explanation: Respiratory epithelium (pseudostratified columnar ciliated epithelium) lines the nasal cavity, pharynx, larynx and trachea.

stem: Which unique feature identifies a cervical vertebra?
options: Heart-shaped body and long spinous process
Large bean-shaped body with interlocking articular facets
Transverse foramen in the transverse process
Costal facets on the body and transverse process
Fused vertebral bodies forming a solid mass
explanation: Cervical vertebrae are identified by their transverse foramina (which transmit the vertebral artery). They also have a short rectangular body, bifid spinous process, and large triangular vertebral foramen. Thoracic vertebrae have costal facets. Lumbar have large bean-shaped bodies.

stem: At what level is a lumbar puncture performed and why is it safe?
options: L1/L2 — at the level of the conus medullaris
Between L2 and L5 — below the conus medullaris, only cauda equina nerve roots present
explanation: Lumbar puncture is performed between L2 and L5 (typically L3/4 or L4/5). The spinal cord ends at L1/L2 as the conus medullaris. Below this only cauda equina nerve roots remain, which can move slightly as the needle enters, preventing damage.

stem: Name the three layers of meninges from superficial to deep and state where CSF is found.
answer: Dura mater (outermost) → arachnoid mater → pia mater (innermost). CSF is found in the subarachnoid space between the arachnoid and pia maters.
explanation: The spinal dura is separated from the vertebral canal by the extradural (epidural) space containing fat, vessels, and loose connective tissue.

stem: Which two muscles invert the foot?
options: Extensor hallucis longus and extensor digitorum longus
Flexor digitorum longus and flexor hallucis longus
explanation: Tibialis anterior (anterior compartment) and tibialis posterior (deep posterior compartment) both invert the foot. Fibularis longus and brevis evert the foot. Inversion and eversion occur at the subtalar joint.

stem: From lateral to medial, what are the contents of the femoral triangle beneath the inguinal ligament?
explanation: NAVY lateral to medial: Nerve, Artery, Vein, Y-fronts (inguinal ligament). The femoral nerve lies outside the femoral sheath; the artery and vein lie within it. The femoral canal (containing lymphatics) lies most medially.

stem: Which nerve passes through the carpal tunnel and what muscles does it supply in the hand?
options: Ulnar nerve — hypothenar muscles and all interossei
Radial nerve — thenar muscles and dorsal hand
Median nerve — thenar muscles (LOAF) and lateral two lumbricals
Median nerve — all intrinsic hand muscles
Anterior interosseous nerve — thenar muscles only
explanation: The median nerve passes through the carpal tunnel beneath the flexor retinaculum. Motor supply via recurrent (thenar) branch: LOAF — Lateral two lumbricals, Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, Flexor pollicis brevis. Sensory to palmar surface of lateral 3.5 digits.

topic: Blood Supply to Head, Neck and Limbs
stem: The dorsalis pedis artery is clinically significant because it is the most distal pulse point from the heart. Where is it located and what is it a continuation of?
options: Posterior to the medial malleolus — continuation of posterior tibial artery
Lateral to extensor hallucis longus on the dorsum of the foot — continuation of anterior tibial artery
In the popliteal fossa — continuation of femoral artery
Between first and second metatarsals — continuation of fibular artery
Medial to extensor digitorum longus — continuation of peroneal artery
explanation: The dorsalis pedis artery is the continuation of the anterior tibial artery at the ankle, lying lateral to the extensor hallucis longus tendon on the dorsum of the foot. It is the most distal pulse point and is used to assess peripheral circulation.

stem: The sciatic nerve exits the pelvis inferior to which muscle?
explanation: The sciatic nerve exits the greater sciatic foramen inferior to piriformis. The superior gluteal NVB exits superior to piriformis. The inferior gluteal NVB exits inferior to piriformis alongside the sciatic nerve. Piriformis is the key landmark.

stem: What are the root values of the phrenic nerve and what is its motor supply?
options: C3/4/5 — all motor innervation to the diaphragm
T1/2/3 — sensory to the diaphragm only
C5/6/7 — motor to diaphragm and intercostals
C3/4/5 — sensory to the diaphragm only
explanation: C3,4,5 keeps the diaphragm alive. The phrenic nerve provides ALL motor innervation to the diaphragm and sensory to the central diaphragm. The peripheral diaphragm receives sensory supply from lower intercostal nerves.

stem: What are the four sites of portocaval anastomosis?
explanation: The four portocaval anastomosis sites: rectum/upper anal canal / distal oesophagus / peri-umbilical region / posterior retroperitoneal viscera. Portal hypertension causes variceal dilatation at these sites — anorectal varices, oesophageal varices (most dangerous), and caput medusae.

stem: Which vessel carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs?
explanation: The pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs. The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood back to the left atrium — the exception to the rule that veins carry deoxygenated blood.
